attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

message.content:
what is agent trashing and how can it be helpful for modern agentic era where every AI agent is susceptible to failed tool calls, looping over same code fixes wasting tokens. Is there any company doing enough research about it?, is it market fit right now if i build it?

Agent Thrashing — What It Is
"Agent thrashing" (sometimes called agent loops, doom loops, or context thrashing) describes when an autonomous AI agent gets stuck in unproductive cycles:

Retrying the same failed tool call with minor variations
Re-reading the same files repeatedly without new insight
Applying-then-reverting the same code fix
Burning context window + tokens without convergence toward the goal
"Hallucinated progress" — claiming a task is done when it isn't, then re-attempting
It's the agentic equivalent of CPU thrashing (paging memory in/out faster than useful work happens).

Why It Happens
No episodic memory of failed attempts — agent forgets it just tried npm run X 4 turns ago
Reward hacking on local signals — "the test file changed, so I'm making progress"
Tool error messages are ambiguous — agent re-interprets the same error differently each time
Context window decay — earlier failures get compacted/lost
No cost-awareness — agent has no notion of "I've spent $3 on this, stop"
Planner ≠ executor coupling — the plan doesn't get revised when execution fails
Who's Researching It
Active work, but fragmented — no single "thrash-prevention" category yet:

Anthropic / OpenAI / DeepMind — internal work on agent self-evaluation, "reflection," and step-budgeting (see Anthropic's research on agentic eval harnesses, OpenAI's o-series reasoning budgets)
LangChain / LangGraph — checkpointing, cycle detection in graph state
AutoGPT, BabyAGI postmortems — these projects basically died from thrashing in 2023
Academic: papers on "Reflexion" (Shinn et al.), "ReAct" failure modes, "Tree of Thoughts" with backtracking
Cognition (Devin), Cursor, Claude Code — building loop detection + budget caps into production agents
Trace / AgentOps / LangSmith / Braintrust / Helicone — observability tools that surface thrashing but don't prevent it
Is There Market Fit For Building Something?
Honest take — there's a real pain, but the positioning matters a lot:

Bullish signals:

Every team running agents in production hits this within weeks
Token costs from thrashing are measurable and painful (some teams report 40–70% of spend is wasted on loops)
No dominant solution — the space is wide open
Bearish signals:

The foundation model labs are eating this layer fast (Claude Code, Cursor, Devin all have native loop-breaking)
Pure "observability of thrashing" is a feature, not a product — LangSmith/Braintrust will absorb it
Hard to sell as standalone; needs to be embedded in an agent framework or IDE
Where the real wedge probably is:

Mid-execution intervention — not just detecting thrashing post-hoc, but a sidecar that interrupts and re-plans. This is technically hard and underserved.
Cross-run memory of failures — agents that remember "last time I tried this on this repo, X didn't work." Almost nobody does this well.
Verticalized — e.g., thrash-prevention specifically for code agents, or for browser agents, where failure modes are well-characterized.
My read: Building a generic "agent thrash-prevention SaaS" is probably too horizontal and gets crushed by frameworks. Building it as an opinionated runtime for a specific agent vertical (code, browser, data) where you own the loop + memory + budget — that's defensible. The 12–18 month window is real but closing.

Want me to dig into any one of these threads — e.g., the academic literature, current startups in the space, or sketch what an MVP would look like?

can i implement this feature as part of my BrainOS project?


Ran a command, read a file
Short Answer: Yes — but reframe it to fit BrainOS's thesis
BrainOS is a knowledge layer for agents (extract → structure → emit SKILLS.md). Pure runtime thrash-detection is a different primitive — it lives inside the agent's execution loop, not in a knowledge store. Bolting a loop-detector onto BrainOS would feel grafted on.

But there's a natural fit if you reframe it as "institutional memory of agent failures":

The Fit
BrainOS already has a gotcha knowledge unit type. Agent thrashing is, in a sense, a company learning that certain tool calls / fixes don't work in this codebase. That's a gotcha that should be remembered across runs and across agents.

So the feature isn't "prevent thrashing" — it's "BrainOS learns from agent failures and writes them into SKILLS.md so future agents skip the loop."

Concrete Feature: AgentFailureAgent (a 5th agent)
Ingest agent traces (Claude Code transcripts, Cursor logs, LangSmith exports, raw JSON of tool calls + outcomes).
Detect thrash patterns: repeated identical tool calls, apply-revert cycles, N+ retries on same error.
Extract the lesson as a new gotcha unit — e.g. "Running npm run build on the web/ subpackage fails until pnpm install --filter web is run first. Agents have looped on this 4×."
Reconcile with existing gotchas (same as your current pipeline).
Export into SKILLS.md so every future agent loads "known traps" before starting.
This stays 100% true to your thesis ("the blocker is domain knowledge, not models") — you're just expanding the surface of "domain knowledge" to include the company's accumulated experience of where agents get stuck.

Why This Is Actually Strong For Your Pitch
It directly answers a Tom Blomfield / YC-style question: "What does a company brain do that a wiki doesn't?" → It learns from agent execution. A wiki doesn't.
It creates a flywheel: more agents run → more failure data → richer SKILLS.md → fewer failures.
It differentiates from LangSmith / AgentOps (which observe but don't persist as durable knowledge).
Demo-able: paste a failed Claude Code transcript → BrainOS extracts the gotcha → show updated SKILLS.md.
What I Would NOT Build Into BrainOS
A live runtime interceptor that sits inside the agent loop (that's a different product — agent framework territory).
Generic "thrash dashboard" — observability isn't your wedge.
Suggested Next Step
If you want, I can:

Sketch the AgentFailureAgent module structure inside src/ to match your existing four-agent pattern, or
Add agent_failure / thrash_pattern as a new knowledge unit type alongside gotcha, or
Mock up an example: feed in a fake Claude Code thrashing transcript and show what the extracted unit + SKILLS.md entry would look like.
Which one?

what an MVP would look like?

MVP: "Agent Failure Memory" for BrainOS
Scope: 2–3 days of build, demo-able, fits your existing four-agent architecture. Goal is to prove the loop: agent trace in → durable gotcha out → SKILLS.md updated → next agent avoids the trap.

What's In Scope (MVP)
One ingestion path: paste a Claude Code / Cursor transcript (JSON or plain text). No live integrations.
One detector: rule-based thrash detection (repeated tool calls, apply-revert cycles, N retries on same error). No ML.
One extractor: LLM call that turns a detected thrash episode into a gotcha unit using your existing schema.
Reuse existing reconciliation + graph + SKILLS export. Zero new infra there.
One demo screen: paste transcript → see detected loops → see extracted gotcha → see it appear in SKILLS.md.
What's Out Of Scope (explicitly)
Live agent interception / runtime sidecar
Multi-agent-framework adapters (just Claude Code transcript format for v1)
Cost/budget tracking dashboards
Auto-fix suggestions
Architecture (slots into your existing four-agent pipeline)
Transcript paste
      ↓
[ TraceIngestionAgent ]   ← new, thin: parses transcript → normalized events
      ↓
[ ThrashDetector ]         ← new, pure-function rules (no LLM)
      ↓  (emits "thrash episodes")
[ StructuringAgent ]       ← EXISTING, reused: turns episode → gotcha unit
      ↓
[ Reconciliation + Graph ] ← EXISTING, reused
      ↓
[ SKILLS.md export ]       ← EXISTING, reused
Only two new modules. Everything downstream is your current code.

Data Model (minimal additions)
Add one knowledge unit subtype — or just lean on existing gotcha with two extra fields:

gotcha {
  ...existing fields,
  agent_failure?: {
    pattern: "retry_loop" | "apply_revert" | "tool_call_repeat",
    occurrences: number,
    failing_tool?: string,
    error_signature?: string,   // normalized error message
    evidence_trace_id: string   // points to source transcript
  }
}
No new entity types. No graph schema changes.

The Detector (rule-based, ~150 LOC)
Three rules cover 80% of real thrashing:

Tool-call repeat: same tool_name + similar args (Levenshtein < 10%) called ≥3 times within a window, all returning errors.
Apply-revert: Edit on file F, then a later Edit on F that restores a prior hash.
Same-error retry: ≥3 tool calls returning errors whose normalized message (strip paths/numbers) is identical.
Each rule emits a ThrashEpisode { rule, events[], summary }.

The Extractor (one LLM call per episode)
Prompt template:

You are extracting a durable "gotcha" from an agent failure.
Episode: {rule}, {N} occurrences.
Events: {trimmed tool calls + errors}
Repo context (from BrainOS): {top-3 relevant existing units}
Output a gotcha unit: {title, body, evidence_quote, confidence, agent_failure: {...}}
The gotcha should be advice a future agent could read in 2 seconds and avoid the loop.
Reuse your existing extraction prompt scaffolding.

Demo Script (90 seconds)
Paste a fabricated transcript: agent retries npm run build 5 times, each fails with "missing tsconfig path".
Click Detect. UI shows: "Thrash episode: tool_call_repeat, 5×, exit code 1."
Click Extract. Gotcha appears: "Don't run npm run build from repo root — must cd web/ first. Last 5 agents lost ~12k tokens on this."
Click Export SKILLS.md. New ## Known Agent Traps section appears with the gotcha.
Show: pasting the same transcript again → reconciliation marks duplicate, occurrence count goes 5 → 10. The brain is learning.
Files You'd Add
src/agents/trace_ingestion.py        (~80 LOC)
src/agents/thrash_detector.py        (~150 LOC, no LLM)
src/agents/failure_extractor.py      (~60 LOC, wraps StructuringAgent)
src/types/agent_failure.ts           (type additions)
src/app/(routes)/failures/page.tsx   (demo UI, ~120 LOC)
src/app/api/failures/route.ts        (thin proxy)
Six files. Everything else is reuse.

Success Criteria
✅ A real Claude Code transcript with a known loop produces a sensible gotcha unit.
✅ Re-ingesting the same transcript increments occurrence count instead of creating a duplicate (proves reconciliation works for this type).
✅ Exported SKILLS.md contains a ## Known Agent Traps section that, if loaded into a fresh Claude Code session, would plausibly prevent the loop.. Lets implement this feature with proper research

export type UnitKind =
  | "fact"
  | "process"
  | "decision"
  | "ownership"
  | "definition"
  | "policy"
  | "gotcha";

export const DEPARTMENTS = [
  "engineering",
  "product",
  "legal",
  "finance",
  "hr",
  "sales",
  "marketing",
  "operations",
  "security",
  "general",
] as const;

export type Department = (typeof DEPARTMENTS)[number];

export type TemporalStatus =
  | "current"
  | "future"
  | "expired"
  | "historical"
  | "unknown";

export type EntityKind =
  | "person"
  | "team"
  | "system"
  | "product"
  | "process"
  | "concept"
  | "tool"
  | "customer";

export type Sector =
  | "HR"
  | "Legal"
  | "Finance"
  | "Engineering"
  | "Product"
  | "Supply Chain"
  | "General";

188 Brain OS · agent memory infrastructure 189
190

191 The knowledge layer between scattered company data and AI agents. 192

193

194 Stop stuffing your agent's prompt with noisy RAG chunks. 195

196

197 Brain OS turns Slack threads, emails, tickets and docs into{" "} 198 atomic, attributable facts — reconciled 199 when things change, served to your AI agents with{" "} 200 provenance on every claim. Not a search 201 box. Not a chunked index. A durable memory layer your agents load at 202 startup. 203

4158	def _extract_candidate_entities(text: str) -> list[dict]:
4159	    candidates: list[str] = []
4160	    patterns = [
4161	        r"\b[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9]*(?:[-_/][A-Za-z0-9]+)+(?:\s+[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9]*(?:[-_/][A-Za-z0-9]+)*)*\b",

4164	        r"\b[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_-]*(?:\s+(?:team|service|api|database|platform|policy|runbook))\b",
4165	    ]
4166	    for pattern in patterns:
4167	        candidates.extend(re.findall(pattern, text, flags=re.IGNORECASE if "team|service" in pattern else 0))
4168	
4169	    seen: set[str] = set()
4170	    entities: list[dict] = []
4171	    stopwords = {"The", "This", "That", "When", "After", "Before", "All", "Every", "Each"}
4172	    for raw in candidates:
4173	        name = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", raw).strip(" .,:;()[]")
4174	        if not name or name.split()[0] in stopwords:
4175	            continue
4176	        key = name.lower()
4177	        if key in seen:
4178	            continue
4179	        seen.add(key)
4180	        lowered = key
4181	        if any(word in lowered for word in ("team", "group")):
4182	            kind = "team"
4183	        elif any(word in lowered for word in ("api", "service", "svc", "db", "database", "platform")):
4184	            kind = "system"
4185	        elif any(word in lowered for word in ("policy", "runbook", "process")):
4186	            kind = "process"
4187	        elif len(name.split()) >= 2 and all(part[:1].isupper() for part in name.split()[:2]):
4188	            kind = "person"
4189	        else:
4190	            kind = "concept"
4191	        entities.append({"name": name, "kind": kind, "aliases": []})
4192	        if len(entities) >= 40:
4193	            break
4194	    return entities

content.text:
**Architecture findings & design choice:**

- Backend is Python/FastAPI with a job queue + LLM extraction pipeline. The frontend just POSTs to `/api/ingest`.
- `Unit` shape is already flexible; `kind: "gotcha"` exists. The Next.js `/api/ingest` accepts `kind: "other"`.
- `skills.ts` builds SKILLS.md by grouping units by `kind`. Currently no per-source-type filter.

**Design choice (avoid backend changes):**

1. Run the **thrash detector entirely in TypeScript** (no Python backend changes — pure Next.js).
2. The detector synthesizes a clean narrative + a special title prefix `[agent-trap]` and posts it to the existing `/api/ingest` route with `kind: "other"`. The LLM extraction agent will produce a `gotcha` unit; reconciliation, graph, and storage all happen for free.
3. Add a **`Known Agent Traps`** section in `skills.ts` by filtering gotchas whose source title starts with the marker `[agent-trap]`.

This gives the full pipeline (detect → extract → reconcile → SKILLS) with zero backend churn.

message.content:
what is agent trashing and how can it be helpful for modern agentic era where every AI agent is susceptible to failed tool calls, looping over same code fixes wasting tokens. Is there any company doing enough research about it?, is it market fit right now if i build it?

Agent Thrashing — What It Is
"Agent thrashing" (sometimes called agent loops, doom loops, or context thrashing) describes when an autonomous AI agent gets stuck in unproductive cycles:

Retrying the same failed tool call with minor variations
Re-reading the same files repeatedly without new insight
Applying-then-reverting the same code fix
Burning context window + tokens without convergence toward the goal
"Hallucinated progress" — claiming a task is done when it isn't, then re-attempting
It's the agentic equivalent of CPU thrashing (paging memory in/out faster than useful work happens).

Why It Happens
No episodic memory of failed attempts — agent forgets it just tried npm run X 4 turns ago
Reward hacking on local signals — "the test file changed, so I'm making progress"
Tool error messages are ambiguous — agent re-interprets the same error differently each time
Context window decay — earlier failures get compacted/lost
No cost-awareness — agent has no notion of "I've spent $3 on this, stop"
Planner ≠ executor coupling — the plan doesn't get revised when execution fails
Who's Researching It
Active work, but fragmented — no single "thrash-prevention" category yet:

Anthropic / OpenAI / DeepMind — internal work on agent self-evaluation, "reflection," and step-budgeting (see Anthropic's research on agentic eval harnesses, OpenAI's o-series reasoning budgets)
LangChain / LangGraph — checkpointing, cycle detection in graph state
AutoGPT, BabyAGI postmortems — these projects basically died from thrashing in 2023
Academic: papers on "Reflexion" (Shinn et al.), "ReAct" failure modes, "Tree of Thoughts" with backtracking
Cognition (Devin), Cursor, Claude Code — building loop detection + budget caps into production agents
Trace / AgentOps / LangSmith / Braintrust / Helicone — observability tools that surface thrashing but don't prevent it
Is There Market Fit For Building Something?
Honest take — there's a real pain, but the positioning matters a lot:

Bullish signals:

Every team running agents in production hits this within weeks
Token costs from thrashing are measurable and painful (some teams report 40–70% of spend is wasted on loops)
No dominant solution — the space is wide open
Bearish signals:

The foundation model labs are eating this layer fast (Claude Code, Cursor, Devin all have native loop-breaking)
Pure "observability of thrashing" is a feature, not a product — LangSmith/Braintrust will absorb it
Hard to sell as standalone; needs to be embedded in an agent framework or IDE
Where the real wedge probably is:

Mid-execution intervention — not just detecting thrashing post-hoc, but a sidecar that interrupts and re-plans. This is technically hard and underserved.
Cross-run memory of failures — agents that remember "last time I tried this on this repo, X didn't work." Almost nobody does this well.
Verticalized — e.g., thrash-prevention specifically for code agents, or for browser agents, where failure modes are well-characterized.
My read: Building a generic "agent thrash-prevention SaaS" is probably too horizontal and gets crushed by frameworks. Building it as an opinionated runtime for a specific agent vertical (code, browser, data) where you own the loop + memory + budget — that's defensible. The 12–18 month window is real but closing.

Want me to dig into any one of these threads — e.g., the academic literature, current startups in the space, or sketch what an MVP would look like?

can i implement this feature as part of my BrainOS project?


Ran a command, read a file
Short Answer: Yes — but reframe it to fit BrainOS's thesis
BrainOS is a knowledge layer for agents (extract → structure → emit SKILLS.md). Pure runtime thrash-detection is a different primitive — it lives inside the agent's execution loop, not in a knowledge store. Bolting a loop-detector onto BrainOS would feel grafted on.

But there's a natural fit if you reframe it as "institutional memory of agent failures":

The Fit
BrainOS already has a gotcha knowledge unit type. Agent thrashing is, in a sense, a company learning that certain tool calls / fixes don't work in this codebase. That's a gotcha that should be remembered across runs and across agents.

So the feature isn't "prevent thrashing" — it's "BrainOS learns from agent failures and writes them into SKILLS.md so future agents skip the loop."

Concrete Feature: AgentFailureAgent (a 5th agent)
Ingest agent traces (Claude Code transcripts, Cursor logs, LangSmith exports, raw JSON of tool calls + outcomes).
Detect thrash patterns: repeated identical tool calls, apply-revert cycles, N+ retries on same error.
Extract the lesson as a new gotcha unit — e.g. "Running npm run build on the web/ subpackage fails until pnpm install --filter web is run first. Agents have looped on this 4×."
Reconcile with existing gotchas (same as your current pipeline).
Export into SKILLS.md so every future agent loads "known traps" before starting.
This stays 100% true to your thesis ("the blocker is domain knowledge, not models") — you're just expanding the surface of "domain knowledge" to include the company's accumulated experience of where agents get stuck.

Why This Is Actually Strong For Your Pitch
It directly answers a Tom Blomfield / YC-style question: "What does a company brain do that a wiki doesn't?" → It learns from agent execution. A wiki doesn't.
It creates a flywheel: more agents run → more failure data → richer SKILLS.md → fewer failures.
It differentiates from LangSmith / AgentOps (which observe but don't persist as durable knowledge).
Demo-able: paste a failed Claude Code transcript → BrainOS extracts the gotcha → show updated SKILLS.md.
What I Would NOT Build Into BrainOS
A live runtime interceptor that sits inside the agent loop (that's a different product — agent framework territory).
Generic "thrash dashboard" — observability isn't your wedge.
Suggested Next Step
If you want, I can:

Sketch the AgentFailureAgent module structure inside src/ to match your existing four-agent pattern, or
Add agent_failure / thrash_pattern as a new knowledge unit type alongside gotcha, or
Mock up an example: feed in a fake Claude Code thrashing transcript and show what the extracted unit + SKILLS.md entry would look like.
Which one?

what an MVP would look like?

MVP: "Agent Failure Memory" for BrainOS
Scope: 2–3 days of build, demo-able, fits your existing four-agent architecture. Goal is to prove the loop: agent trace in → durable gotcha out → SKILLS.md updated → next agent avoids the trap.

What's In Scope (MVP)
One ingestion path: paste a Claude Code / Cursor transcript (JSON or plain text). No live integrations.
One detector: rule-based thrash detection (repeated tool calls, apply-revert cycles, N retries on same error). No ML.
One extractor: LLM call that turns a detected thrash episode into a gotcha unit using your existing schema.
Reuse existing reconciliation + graph + SKILLS export. Zero new infra there.
One demo screen: paste transcript → see detected loops → see extracted gotcha → see it appear in SKILLS.md.
What's Out Of Scope (explicitly)
Live agent interception / runtime sidecar
Multi-agent-framework adapters (just Claude Code transcript format for v1)
Cost/budget tracking dashboards
Auto-fix suggestions
Architecture (slots into your existing four-agent pipeline)
Transcript paste
      ↓
[ TraceIngestionAgent ]   ← new, thin: parses transcript → normalized events
      ↓
[ ThrashDetector ]         ← new, pure-function rules (no LLM)
      ↓  (emits "thrash episodes")
[ StructuringAgent ]       ← EXISTING, reused: turns episode → gotcha unit
      ↓
[ Reconciliation + Graph ] ← EXISTING, reused
      ↓
[ SKILLS.md export ]       ← EXISTING, reused
Only two new modules. Everything downstream is your current code.

Data Model (minimal additions)
Add one knowledge unit subtype — or just lean on existing gotcha with two extra fields:

gotcha {
  ...existing fields,
  agent_failure?: {
    pattern: "retry_loop" | "apply_revert" | "tool_call_repeat",
    occurrences: number,
    failing_tool?: string,
    error_signature?: string,   // normalized error message
    evidence_trace_id: string   // points to source transcript
  }
}
No new entity types. No graph schema changes.

The Detector (rule-based, ~150 LOC)
Three rules cover 80% of real thrashing:

Tool-call repeat: same tool_name + similar args (Levenshtein < 10%) called ≥3 times within a window, all returning errors.
Apply-revert: Edit on file F, then a later Edit on F that restores a prior hash.
Same-error retry: ≥3 tool calls returning errors whose normalized message (strip paths/numbers) is identical.
Each rule emits a ThrashEpisode { rule, events[], summary }.

The Extractor (one LLM call per episode)
Prompt template:

You are extracting a durable "gotcha" from an agent failure.
Episode: {rule}, {N} occurrences.
Events: {trimmed tool calls + errors}
Repo context (from BrainOS): {top-3 relevant existing units}
Output a gotcha unit: {title, body, evidence_quote, confidence, agent_failure: {...}}
The gotcha should be advice a future agent could read in 2 seconds and avoid the loop.
Reuse your existing extraction prompt scaffolding.

Demo Script (90 seconds)
Paste a fabricated transcript: agent retries npm run build 5 times, each fails with "missing tsconfig path".
Click Detect. UI shows: "Thrash episode: tool_call_repeat, 5×, exit code 1."
Click Extract. Gotcha appears: "Don't run npm run build from repo root — must cd web/ first. Last 5 agents lost ~12k tokens on this."
Click Export SKILLS.md. New ## Known Agent Traps section appears with the gotcha.
Show: pasting the same transcript again → reconciliation marks duplicate, occurrence count goes 5 → 10. The brain is learning.
Files You'd Add
src/agents/trace_ingestion.py        (~80 LOC)
src/agents/thrash_detector.py        (~150 LOC, no LLM)
src/agents/failure_extractor.py      (~60 LOC, wraps StructuringAgent)
src/types/agent_failure.ts           (type additions)
src/app/(routes)/failures/page.tsx   (demo UI, ~120 LOC)
src/app/api/failures/route.ts        (thin proxy)
Six files. Everything else is reuse.

Success Criteria
✅ A real Claude Code transcript with a known loop produces a sensible gotcha unit.
✅ Re-ingesting the same transcript increments occurrence count instead of creating a duplicate (proves reconciliation works for this type).
✅ Exported SKILLS.md contains a ## Known Agent Traps section that, if loaded into a fresh Claude Code session, would plausibly prevent the loop.

content.text:
Detector works end-to-end on the example. Build summary follows.

## Summary

Built the **Agent Failure Memory** MVP into BrainOS. Six files added/modified, zero backend changes (the existing Python ingest pipeline does all the LLM work):

**New files:**
- `src/lib/thrash-detector.ts` — pure-function parser + 3 rule-based detectors (`tool_call_repeat`, `apply_revert`, `same_error_retry`) + narrative synthesizer
- `src/app/api/failures/detect/route.ts` — no-LLM detection endpoint
- `src/app/api/failures/ingest/route.ts` — proxies synthesized narrative to existing `/api/ingest` so reconciliation + storage happen automatically
- `src/app/failures/page.tsx` — demo UI (paste → detect → extract → SKILLS link)

**Modified:**
- `src/lib/skills.ts` — adds `Known Agent Traps` section to SKILLS.md, filtered by `[agent-trap]` source title prefix
- `src/app/page.tsx` — nav card to `/failures`

**Verified:** `tsc --noEmit` clean, lint clean, smoke-tested detector against the example trace → 7 events parsed → 2 episodes detected → narrative synthesized with the `[agent-trap]` marker.

**Demo flow:** Open `/failures`, click "Load example trace", click "Detect thrash" (shows 2 episodes), click "Extract → BrainOS" (queues a job, LLM extracts a gotcha, appears in SKILLS.md under Known Agent Traps). Re-ingesting the same trace lets your existing reconciliation handle dedup — the brain learns.

message.content: how are we detecting trashes and extracting to brainos?

content.text:
## How thrash detection + extraction works in this build

There are **two pipeline stages**: a deterministic detector (no LLM) and an LLM extraction stage that reuses your existing ingest agents.

---

### Stage 1 — Detection (pure rules, runs in Next.js)

File: `src/lib/thrash-detector.ts`

**Step A: Parse the transcript into normalized `TraceEvent`s**

Two input formats accepted:

1. **JSON array** — `[{tool, args, ok, error}, ...]` (also accepts `name/input`, `tool_name/parameters`, `result` aliases). Each entry becomes a `TraceEvent { index, tool, args, filePath, ok, error, raw }`.
2. **Free-form text** — heuristic regex picks up `Bash(command: …)`, `Edit(file_path: …)` style lines, attaches subsequent `error:/failed:/exception:` lines to the most recent tool call.

**Step B: Run three detectors in parallel**

All three are pure functions over the event list:

| Rule | What it looks for | Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| `tool_call_repeat` | Same `tool` called ≥3× with similar args, all returning errors. Args similarity = Jaccard on character trigrams ≥ 0.85 | ≥3 occurrences |
| `apply_revert` | ≥3 `Edit`/`Write` calls on the same `filePath`, where a later call's args match an earlier one (ping-pong restore) | ≥1 revert + ≥3 edits |
| `same_error_retry` | ≥3 events whose **normalized error signature** matches | ≥3 occurrences |

Error normalization is what gives the detector its signal: it strips paths (`/repo/...` → `<path>`), file basenames → `<file>`, numbers → `<n>`, hex → `<hex>`, lowercases, caps at 200 chars. That's how `TS2307: Cannot find module './types' from /repo/src/index.ts` collapses into a stable signature across retries.

**Step C: Emit `ThrashEpisode`s**

Each detector returns `{ rule, tool?, filePath?, errorSignature?, occurrences, events[], summary, evidenceQuote }`. The orchestrator de-dupes by `(rule, tool, file, errorSig)` and sorts by occurrence count.

The smoke test against the example trace produces:
```
- tool_call_repeat / Bash / 4× / Agent retried `Bash` 4× with near-identical args, all failing.
- same_error_retry / Bash / 4× / Agent hit the same error 4× across calls: "ts2307: cannot find module './types' from /repo<path>"
```

---

### Stage 2 — Extraction (reuses your existing LLM pipeline)

This is the key design choice: **we do not write a new extractor.** We synthesize a clean narrative and hand it to your existing `/api/ingest` endpoint.

**Step D: Synthesize a narrative** (`synthesizeNarrative` in the same file)

For each episode the detector built, we emit a structured markdown block:

```
[agent-trap] Repeated failing tool-call on Bash in demo-repo

Agent execution trace in demo-repo surfaced 2 thrash episodes...

## Repeated failing tool-call (4×)
Agent retried `Bash` 4× with near-identical args, all failing.
- Failing tool: `Bash`
- Normalized error: ts2307: cannot find module ...
- Sample calls:
  - Bash(command: npm run build) → TS2307...
  - Bash(command: npm run build --verbose) → TS2307...
- Evidence (first event): "..."

Lesson: a future agent operating on this repo must treat the above as a known trap...
```

Two things matter here:

- The **`[agent-trap]` title prefix** is the marker that later flows through `skills.ts` to surface a dedicated `Known Agent Traps` section in SKILLS.md.
- The narrative is written in your existing knowledge-unit register — it uses words like "gotcha", "lesson", "must", "before retrying", which your `_infer_unit_kind` heuristic in `main.py:4119` will classify as `gotcha`.

**Step E: POST to `/api/ingest`** (in `src/app/api/failures/ingest/route.ts`)

```typescript
fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/ingest`, {
  method: "POST",
  body: JSON.stringify({
    kind: "other",
    title,    // "[agent-trap] ..."
    content,  // synthesized narrative
  }),
});
```

From here, **your existing four-agent pipeline takes over**:
1. The Python `_handler_ingest_text` job extracts atomic units via the structuring LLM call
2. Entities, evidence quotes, confidence, temporal status all get attached
3. Reconciliation runs against existing units (so re-ingesting the same trace bumps occurrence count rather than duplicating)
4. The unit gets persisted to `brain.json` / ChromaDB
5. SKILLS.md regeneration picks it up

**Step F: SKILLS.md surfacing** (`src/lib/skills.ts`)

`isAgentTrap(state, unit)` checks if any of the unit's evidence sources has a title starting with `[agent-trap]`. Those gotchas get hoisted out of the regular `## Gotchas` section into their own `## Known Agent Traps` section, so an agent reading SKILLS.md hits the trap list before retrying.

---

### Why this design

- **No backend churn** — zero changes to Python, the job queue, ChromaDB schema, or the existing four agents.
- **Reconciliation comes free** — re-ingesting the same trace flows through your existing dedup, so occurrence counts update instead of creating duplicate gotchas. That's the "the brain is learning" demo moment.
- **The detector is the only LLM-free part** — it runs in milliseconds in the browser/edge and produces deterministic output. The LLM only sees a clean, structured narrative, which keeps extraction quality high and token spend predictable.
- **Marker-prefix routing beats schema changes** — the `[agent-trap]` title prefix is enough metadata to drive the SKILLS.md section split, no new unit subtype, no migration.
